changelog:
Ava remains first a **voice assistant**, and this update opens that direction much wider. Not every device is best suited to running a full voice pipeline all day, and not every user wants one device locked into a single role forever. Some want a voice terminal, others prefer a quiet browser panel, a screensaver device, or a stable music endpoint. Ava now embraces that difference much more naturally.

For browser-based voice, **voice-satellite-card-integration** is genuinely impressive. Its interface feels modern, clean, and direct. This release opens that path further and gives users more freedom in how they want to use their hardware.

A new **Voice Channel** master switch has been added. You can now directly control Ava's full native voice pipeline from voice settings. When enabled, Ava keeps its complete original voice capabilities. When disabled, voice input, wake word handling, voice assistant threads, and related entities all stop together, so both the interface and the background behavior become cleaner and lighter.

This matters because browser WebRTC voice and Ava's native voice pipeline can compete for microphone resources on some devices. If you want to prioritize browser voice, you can now manually disable Ava's voice channel. The device stays quieter, lighter, and more power-efficient. I also hope the browser voice route continues to improve further, especially with a future manual wake entry similar to Ava Assistant. Home Assistant's own native voice assistant already handles this part very well.

The built-in browser microphone path has also been strengthened. WebRTC handling is now much more open, and Ava's internal browser is no longer just a simple webpage container. It is becoming a much more practical place to host browser-based voice interfaces directly. This is especially useful for hardware that works better as a long-running browser terminal with stable display, low idle power use, and reliable always-on presence.

**Sendspin** keeps getting better. As Ava's music terminal protocol, it has become smoother, stronger, and more practical to use. Many devices do not need to listen for wake words all the time, but they can still become excellent music endpoints. With the music pipeline now opened up even further, switching a device into that role feels far more natural.

That flexibility is exciting. A device can be used for voice, or it can quietly serve as a music endpoint, a screensaver panel, a browser dashboard, or a stable visual terminal. Open, flexible, power-efficient, and still very capable. That is exactly the direction Ava should grow toward.

Alongside these improvements, the **Mod Store** is becoming more important too. Ava is no longer just a fixed-function app. It is gradually becoming a more open platform that can continue to expand. Through the Mod Store, more device adaptations, UI ideas, control logic, and new feature paths can keep arriving over time. This gives Ava a much longer life and gives users more freedom to shape it around their own hardware and workflows.

This release also fixes the browser pull-to-refresh behavior being too sensitive. Previously, dragging down inside normal page content could trigger a refresh too easily. Refresh now triggers much more strictly from the top region only, so scrolling and refreshing feel clearer and much more natural.

If you are using a local Home Assistant address such as `http://192.168.0.xxx:8123/` and want browser voice, WebRTC, microphone access, and other secure-context features to work more reliably, moving to **HTTPS** is strongly recommended. Prepare a certificate and private key, add an `http` section in `configuration.yaml` pointing to these files, restart Home Assistant, and switch to `https://your-address:8123/`. If the certificate is not trusted, install it manually. For browser voice, WebRTC, and microphone capture, HTTPS remains the more reliable path.

Let Ava truly become more free. Let each device do what it is best at.

by knoop7
